coredata.dc:title: High potential and robust ternary LaFeO3/CdS/carbon quantum dots nanocomposite for photocatalytic H2 evolution under sunlight illumination

coredata.dc:description: Exploitation of the novel, robust, and advanced photocatalytic systems with high efficiency is the present demand for clean, green, and sustainable energy production. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted tremendous interest in efficient H2 evolution from photocatalysis due to its remarkable visible-light harvesting and electron transport properties. Here, for the first time, a smart ternary nanocomposite comprises encapsulated CQDs with LaFeO3 spherical nanoparticles and CdS nanorods is synthesized by a simple hydrothermal procedure for the efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution under sunlight illumination. PXRD, FT-IR, FE-SEM, TEM, and XPS studies are performed to ensure the successful fabrication of ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite. The efficient H2 evolution rate (HER) of 25,302 μmol h−1 gcat −1 is achieved for LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite, which is 602.4, 2.6, 29.8, 2.0 and 1.1 times higher than that of pristine LaFeO3, pristine CdS, and composites such as LaFeO3/CdS, LaFeO3/CQD, and CdS/CQD. Photocurrent and lifetime PL studies reveal, encapsulation of CQDs with the LaFeO3/CdS heterojunction can facilitate easy and efficient separation of photo-generated excitons. Altogether the fabrication of CQDs provides an ideal avenue for the development of high potential advanced photocatalytic systems for sustainable H2 production with remarkable efficiencies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PXRD Studies (a), FT-IR Studies (b), UV–Visible studies (c), HR-TEM (d) and SAED (e) micrographs and corresponding size distribution graph (f) of synthesized CQDs.

FE-SEM images of pristine LaFeO3 (a), pristine CdS (b), and LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite (c) and EDS pattern and corresponding elemental mapping images (d) of LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite in the selected area.
TEM images (a-i) of ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite.
HR-TEM images (a-b), and SAED pattern (c) of ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite.

PXRD patterns (a), FT-IR spectra (b), UV–Vis-DRS Studies (c) and Tauc plots (d) of synthesized pristine LaFeO3, and pristine CdS, and nanocomposites of LaFeO3/CQD, CdS/CQD, LaFeO3/CdS, and LaFeO3/CdS/CQD.

XS survey spectrum of the as-synthesized LaFeO3, CdS, CQDs, and LaFeO3/CdS/CQD samples (a), and the attributed high-resolution spectra of La 3d (b), Fe 2p (c), Cd 3d (d), S 2p (e), C 1s (f), and O 1s (g).

Comparative study of HERs (a), photocatalytic H2 evolution for 4 h (b) over synthesized pristine LaFeO3, pristine CdS, LaFeO3/CQD, CdS/CQD, LaFeO3/CdS, LaFeO3/CdS/CQD photocatalysts under solar light illumination, photocurrent experiments (c), and time-resolved PL studies (d) of synthesized LaFeO3/CQD, CdS/CQD, LaFeO3/CdS, LaFeO3/CdS/CQD photocatalysts.
Schematic depiction of photocatalytic H2 production from aqueous lactic acid solution over binary LaFeO3/CdS (a) and ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD (b) nanocomposites under sunlight illumination.
Illustration for the synthesis of ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite.
Comparison of photocatalytic HER of binary LaFeO3/CQD, CdS/CQD, LaFeO3/CdS, and ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposites with the other recently reported LaFeO3-based and CdS-based photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: First time a highly robust ternary LaFeO3/CdS/carbon quantum dots nanocomposite is designed with good interfacial interactions and optoelectronic properties for efficient H2 evolution (25,302 μmol h−1 gcat −1) from solar-light driven water splitting.

Exploitation of the novel, robust, and advanced photocatalytic systems with high efficiency is the present demand for clean, green, and sustainable energy production. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted tremendous interest in efficient H2 evolution from photocatalysis due to its remarkable visible-light harvesting and electron transport properties. Here, for the first time, a smart ternary nanocomposite comprises encapsulated CQDs with LaFeO3 spherical nanoparticles and CdS nanorods is synthesized by a simple hydrothermal procedure for the efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution under sunlight illumination. PXRD, FT-IR, FE-SEM, TEM, and XPS studies are performed to ensure the successful fabrication of ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite. The efficient H2 evolution rate (HER) of 25,302 μmol h−1 gcat −1 is achieved for LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite, which is 602.4, 2.6, 29.8, 2.0 and 1.1 times higher than that of pristine LaFeO3, pristine CdS, and composites such as LaFeO3/CdS, LaFeO3/CQD, and CdS/CQD. Photocurrent and lifetime PL studies reveal, encapsulation of CQDs with the LaFeO3/CdS heterojunction can facilitate easy and efficient separation of photo-generated excitons. Altogether the fabrication of CQDs provides an ideal avenue for the development of high potential advanced photocatalytic systems for sustainable H2 production with remarkable efficiencies.

ce:section.ce:para: World needs twice and thrice to its energy supply by 2050 and 2100 [1]. Subsequently, environmental pollution and global warming are also increasing due to the uncontrolled use of fossil fuels. To alleviate these issues, there is an utmost need to develop affordable and accessible sustainable technologies to produce clean and green energy (H2) from H2O. Nevertheless, the splitting of H2O is unfavourable due to its thermo dynamical stability (ΔG0 = +237 KJ/mol) [2,3]. In this context, clean and green energy production by splitting water with semiconductor material and sunlight is the most promising and green sustainable avenue [4]. Since, the first report on photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting on TiO2 photoelectrode by Fujishima and Honda in 1953, a lot of robust strategies have been pursued for an effective solar light harvesting photocatalysts for efficient H2 production [5–7]. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of the semiconductor photocatalysts is still holding back from their commercial application due to the high rate of recombination of photo-generated excitons and narrow solar spectral response [8]. Therefore, a realization of a new-class of visible-light-active materials with high efficiency is pivotal on a large-scale practical application for photocatalytic H2 evolution.

Recent studies revealed that LaFeO3 (LFO) is the common perovskite material with ABO3 formula has received considerable attention due to its wonderful properties including narrow bandgap (2.1 eV) [9], and potential applications such as photocatalysis [10], sensors [11], fuel cells [12], and catalysis [13]. However, LaFeO3 suffers from certain shortcomings such as low surface area, fast charge carrier recombination, which leads to low efficiency [9]. To address these concerns, several strategies have been discussed such as doping with metals/non-metals such as B-doped [14] and Ru-doped LaFeO3 [15], Ni decorated LaFeO3 [16], morphology modification like LaFeO3 flowers [17], and forming composites with semiconductors like g-C3N4 [18] and TiO2 [19]. Among these, the synthesis of semiconductor–semiconductor nanocomposites is an efficient approach to improve the efficiency [20], where we can suppress the recombination rate of photo-generated excitons at the interface of two semiconductors.

In another hand, CdS (CS) is one of the special chalcogenide materials that has gained significant attention owing to its extraordinary applications including solar cells [21], photocatalysis [22], light-emitting diodes [23], sensors [24], photovoltaics [25], drug delivery [26], and anti-bacterial applications [27]. Since it has a small bandgap (2.4 eV) ability to absorb visible light [28] and offers an easy way to control size and morphology [29]. But CdS also suffers from high recombination and photostability as like LaFeO3. In this context, to improve the stability and efficiency of the photocatalysts both LaFeO3 and CdS, it is a better idea to make LaFeO3/CdS binary nanocomposite. There is still a great deal of work to be done to improve the photocatalytic H2 evolution.

Although an artificial LaFeO3/CdS binary photocatalytic system comprising of two narrow bandgap semiconductors experience inefficiency in charge separation. To promote high efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution, it is proved that co-catalyst modification with carbon nanomaterials is a productive method, which can provide not only a high surface area and numerous reaction sites but also enhances visible-light absorption, further stimulating the interfacial charge transfer [30]. Hence, it is important to choose a low-cost, hazardous-less, stable carbon nanomaterials as co-catalyst to combine with LaFeO3/CdS binary nanocomposite. Until now, a few electron transferring mediums have been exploited to design novel and high efficient ternary photocatalytic systems including grapheme [31,32]. carbon nanotubes [33], nanocarbon [34], carbon quantum dots [35], carbon nanospheres [36] for H2 evolution. Among these, CQDs, a distinctive class of nano-sized carbon materials with less than 10 nm first discovered by Xu et al in 2004 and have been extensively investigated in photocatalysis, bio-imaging, solar cells, light-emitting diodes, supercapacitors, sensors, drug delivery, biomedicines, organic synthesis due to its good water solubility, less toxicity, high chemical, and photostability, extraordinary optical and luminescent properties [37–39].

In the current study, for the first time, we are reporting a simple hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots based LaFeO3/CdS ternary nanocomposite (LaFeO3/CdS/CQD) and its application towards the highly efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution. The developed LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite has shown excellent photocatalytic H2 evolution, when compared to pristine LaFeO3, pristine CdS, and composites including LaFeO3/CdS, LaFeO3/CQD, and CdS/CQD under sunlight illumination. A plausible mechanism has also been proposed for the efficient H2 evolution of LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite.

CQDs were synthesized by using a simple microwave method. In brief, 1:2:2 ratios of sucrose (100 mg), fructose (200 mg), and glucose (200 mg) were dissolved in 5 mL of double distilled (DD) water. Then 5 mL of PEG-600 was added and mixed homogeneously. Further, 40 mL of DD water was added to the prepared solution and put into a 600 W microwave oven for 10 min. Finally, the obtained black color suspensions of CQDs are centrifuged several times and dried the supernatant by using a vacuum pump via the freeze-drying process.

LaFeO3 was synthesized by using CTAB assisted hydrothermal synthesis. In brief, La(NO3)3 (1 mmol) and K4Fe(CN)6 (1 mmol) were mixed into 40 mL of ethylene glycol and stirred to obtain a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, CTAB (3 mmol) was added to that solution and stirred for 30 min. Afterward, the suspension was poured into 50 mL capacity autoclave and treated hydrothermally at 180 °C for 12 h. Finally, the obtained wine red color precipitate was washed several times with ethanol and dried in an oven at 80 °C for overnight. The dried powder was calcined at 800 °C for 2 h.

CdS nanorods were synthesized by using a previously reported simple hydrothermal process [40]. In brief, 0.13 M of cadmium chloride was dissolved in 40 mL of DD water. Then 0.73 M of thiourea was added to the above solution and stirred for 1 h. The obtained clear solution was collected in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and treated hydrothermally at 180 °C for 24 h. Finally, the obtained orange color precipitate was centrifuged and washed with DD water and dried in an oven at 80 °C overnight.

Ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite was synthesized by using a simple hydrothermal process. In brief, the appropriate amount of synthesized CQDs (5 wt%) was added to the beaker containing 40 mL of DD water and sonicated for 15 min. Further, 1:1 ratio of synthesized LaFeO3 (50 mg) and CdS (50 mg) were added and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, the obtained solution was collected in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and treated hydrothermally at 180 °C for 6 h. Thus the obtained precipitate was centrifuged and washed with DD water. Finally, the precipitate was dried in an oven at 80 °C overnight. A similar procedure was followed for the synthesis of LaFeO3/CdS, LaFeO3/CQD and CdS/CQD hybrid nanocomposites in the absence of CQDs, CdS, and LaFeO3 respectively. The synthesis of binary LaFeO3/CdS and ternary carbon quantum dots based LaFeO3/CdS nanocomposites were illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The part of the experimental details including characterization techniques, photo-electrochemical experiments, and photocatalytic H2 evolution tests used in this work was provided in the supporting information.

The structural, optical, and morphological characteristics of synthesized CQDs were verified by PXRD, FT-IR, UV–Visible, TEM, and HR-TEM studies, as shown in Fig. 1 a-f. Fig. 1a contains a typical PXRD pattern of CQDs and graphite powder for the comparison. CQDs reflect a wide peak positioned at around 24.06°, corresponding to the (002) plane with a dspacing of 0.364 nm [41]. The less peak intensity and more full width half maximum (FWHM) are corresponding to the presence of oxygen functionalities on the surface of CQDs and the small size of the CQDs.

Fig. 1b consists of, FT-IR spectra of CQDs. It describes one broadband at around 3400–3173 and 1623 cm−1 correspond to the –OH stretching and bending vibrations. The small bands at around 2925 and 2852 cm−1 attributed to CH stretching vibrations. Fig. 1c represents UV–Visible studies of CQDs. It shows two characteristic bands at 260 and 287 nm corresponding to π-π* and n-π* transitions of an aromatic system [42]. Fig. 1d and 1f display, HR-TEM image and size distribution graphs of CQDs. They were seemed to be small spherical particles and distributed uniformly size ranging from 2 to 5 nm with an average size (d) of 3.4 nm. Fig. 1e shows the SAED pattern of synthesized CQDs reveals the characteristic (002) and (101) planes, which directly correlates to the PXRD pattern. Further, the synthesized CQDs were fabricated with LaFeO3 and CdS materials.

The morphological and nano-structural characteristics of the synthesized pristine LaFeO3, CdS, and LaFeO3/CdS/CQD materials were examined by FE-SEM and TEM studies. As exhibited in Fig. 2 a, LaFeO3 possessed a spherical morphology that comprised a few randomly disposed of spherical nanoparticles. CdS (Fig. 2b) displays rod-like nanostructures that were also randomly oriented. The EDS of LaFeO3 and CdS (Figure S1a and S1b)) exhibit the presence of La, Fe, O and Cd, S elements. The FE-SEM images of ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite (Fig. 2c) show LaFeO3 and CdS components and CQDs were not observed clearly due to their low average size. Besides, the EDS pattern of ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite (Fig. 2d) comprises La, Fe, Cd, S, N, O, and C elements confirms the coexistence of CQDs along with LaFeO3 and CdS. Additionally, the corresponding EDS elemental mapping of ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite consists of homogeneously distributed La, Fe, Cd, S, N, O, and C elements and which further strengthen the successful encapsulation of CQDs into the LaFeO3/CdS nanocomposite. Thus, the EDS pattern and elemental mapping results endorse the development of ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite.

As displayed in Fig. 3 a-i, the rod-like CdS nanostructures and LaFeO3 spherical nanostructures have coexisted together and CQDs are well deposited on them. Further, the HR-TEM images (Fig. 4 a-b) of LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite exhibits lattice spacing of about 0.284 and 0.334 nm corresponding to the (110) plane of LaFeO3 and the (002) plane of CdS, respectively. SAED pattern of LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite is represented in Fig. 4c. These results clarify both hierarchical morphology and nature of surface-surface interaction between the three components in LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite can lead to excellent photocatalytic H2 evolution.

Fig. 5 a consists of the PXRD studies of as-synthesized materials. The pristine LaFeO3 material shows the characteristic diffraction planes of [(100), (110), (111), (200), (211), (220), and (310) planes at 2θ = 22.7°, 32.2°, 39.7°, 46.2°, 57.4°, 67.4°, and 76.7°] cubic perovskite phase (JCPDS No: 75–0541) [43] and the pristine CdS shows the characteristic diffraction planes of [(100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (202), (203), (210), (211), (114), and (105) at 2θ = 24.9°, 26.6°, 28.3°, 36.7°, 43.8°, 48.0°, 51.0°, 51.9°, 52.9°, 58.4°, 66.9°, 69.4°, 70.9°, 72.6°, and 75.6°] hexagonal phase (JCPDS No: 89–2944) [44]. And the corresponding JCPDS standard patterns are represented in Figure S2a and S2b of supporting information. Moreover, all the nanocomposites show all characteristic diffraction planes of the individual components corresponding to their composition. But in the case of LaFeO3/CQD, CdS/CQD, and LaFeO3/CdS/CQD materials the characteristic diffraction plane of CQDs is not detected clearly, due to low crystallinity and homogeneous distribution of CQDs [45].

Fig. 5b consists of the FT-IR spectra of as-synthesized materials. In all materials, the broadband around 3000–3700 and 1624 cm−1 corresponding to the adsorbed H2O/OH molecules stretching and bending vibrations, respectively. The pristine LaFeO3 shows two strong characteristic bands situated at around 432 and 635 cm−1 were corresponding to the O–Fe–O bond bending and Fe–O stretching frequencies [10]. The pristine CdS shows one characteristic band at 652 cm−1 assigned to the Cd–S bond [46]. In the case of nanocomposites, all characteristic bands present respective to their composition.

The light-response properties of all the synthesized materials were deliberated by conducting UV–Vis DRS studies. As displayed in Fig. 5c, LaFeO3 exhibits an absorption edge approximately at 590 nm corresponding to the electronic transition from valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) i.e; from O(2p) to the Fe(3d) and for CdS exhibit an absorption edge approximately at 536 nm corresponding to the electronic transition from VB to CB i.e; from S(3p) to the Cd(5 s)(5p) indicating their visible-light absorption properties [17,47]. Additionally, the corresponding bandgap energies of LaFeO3 and CdS were calculated to be 2.10 and 2.31 eV according to Tauc plots (Fig. 5d) and LaFeO3/CdS binary nanocomposite displays a blue shift with an absorption edge around at 505 nm, indicating co-existence of LaFeO3 and CdS materials. A slight red shift was observed in the absorption edge after encapsulation of CQDs with LaFeO3, CdS, and LaFeO3/CdS/CQD, stipulating the improved visible-light absorption efficiency. These distinctively enhanced optical characteristics of LaFeO3/CdS, LaFeO3/CQD, CdS/CQD, LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposites could generate a high amount of photo-induced excitons required for the photocatalytic reaction, which can result in excellent H2 evolution performances.

Fig. 6a represents the XPS spectra of as-synthesized pristine LaFeO3, pristine CdS, CQDs, and LaFeO3/CdS/CQD photocatalysts find the existence of La, Fe, O; Cd, S; and La, Fe, Cd, S, O, C elements, respectively. In high-resolution XPS spectra of LaFeO3, as shown in Fig. 6b finds the two peaks at 832.86 and 849.45 eV, correlate with the characteristic binding energies (BEs) of the spin–orbit splitting of La 3d5/2 and La 3d3/2 of La3+. Fig. 6c finds the three peaks at 709.70, 717.4, and 723.31 eV, agree with the characteristic BEs of Fe 2p3/2, satellite peak, and Fe 2p1/2 of Fe3+. The core-level spectra of La 3d and Fe 2p disclose that the La, Fe atoms are in the + 3 valence state and in the forms of oxides [17]. Further, in high-resolution XPS spectra of CdS, as shown in Fig. 6d finds the two peaks at 404.56 and 411.29 eV, attributed to the characteristic BEs of Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 of Cd2+. The high-resolution spectrum of S 2p as displayed in Fig. 6e can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 161.14 and 162.32 eV, attributed to the characteristic BEs of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of S2− [48]. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s as exhibited in Fig. 6g can be elucidated into three peaks at 528.65, 530.73, and 532.84 eV, ascribed to the characteristic BEs of O2– of La–O and Fe–O present in the LFO lattice (Olatt), O2– of oxygen defects/vacancies (Ovac), and O2– of physi/chemisorbed OH/H2O on the surface (Oads) [49].

Besides, the relative peak intensity of Ovac in the ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite has been increased compared to that of LaFeO3; this signifies the occurrence of a large amount of oxygen defects/vacancies. However, recent studies revealed that the existence of oxygen vacancies in the photocatalysts can be influence the photocatalytic activity by suppressing the excitons recombination, effectively [50,51]. The existence of CQDs in LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite was also further endorsed by XPS studies. Fig. 6 f showed the very broad and asymmetrical peak of C 1 s, constructs with four peaks with the BEs of 283.72, 285.57, 287.60, and 291.85 eV corresponding to the sp2 hybridized (CC) carbon, epoxy/hydroxyl (CO) carbon, carbonyl (CO) carbon, and shake-up satellite due to vacuum contamination [52]. In the LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite, it is clearly distinguished that the peaks of La 3d, Fe 2p, Cd 3d, Ovac, S 2p, and C 1 s shift towards lower BE side. These may be due to strong interactions among the LaFeO3, CdS, and CQDs materials which implies partial electron transfer among the three materials [53]. Thus, XPS results describe the co-existence of LaFeO3, CdS, and CQDs in the LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite, which directly concurs with the EDS and elemental mapping studies.

The photocatalytic capabilities of the synthesized photocatalysts were tested for H2 evolution from water under sunlight illumination using the lactic acid-aqueous solution as a sacrificial agent. As mentioned in Fig. 7 a, pristine LaFeO3, pristine CdS, and nanocomposites of LaFeO3/CQD, CdS/CQD, LaFeO3/CdS, and LaFeO3/CdS/CQD were shown photocatalytic HERs of 42, 9,604, 849, 12,775, 22,537 and 25,302 μmol h−1 gcat −1, respectively. The presence of ultra-small CQDs synergistically enhances the photocatalytic activity of LaFeO3 and CdS under solar light illumination in the case of LaFeO3/CQD and CdS/CQD nanocomposites. Further, LaFeO3/CdS binary nanocomposite encapsulated ultra-small CQDs (LaFeO3/CdS/CQD) extensively enhance the photocatalytic HER and effectively promote charge separation compare to that of LaFeO3/CdS nanocomposite. Additionally, the synergetic effects between the three components present in the smart architecture of the ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite can also responsible for the excellent photocatalytic H2 evolution. The average HER for LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite was 602.4, 2.6, 29.8, 2.0 and 1.1 times higher than that of pristine LaFeO3, pristine CdS, LaFeO3/CQD, CdS/CQD, and LaFeO3/CdS, respectively. These results clearly attributed that the encapsulation of CQDs with pristine LaFeO3, pristine CdS, and LaFeO3/CdS nanocomposite act as electron mediators/co-catalysts to improve solar-light harvesting, and photo-induced effective charge separation which further enhance the HER significantly. Fig. 7b, the rates of H2 production by synthesized samples were recorded and exhibited an apparent increase during the 4 h of solar light illumination, demonstrating their stability and reproducibility of the HERs over synthesized samples. From Table 1 , it can be confirmed that the designed ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite was exhibited an excellent photocatalytic H2 evolution than the majority of other previously reported LaFeO3 and CdS-based photocatalysts, which demonstrates that the developed ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite can be considered as high potential photocatalyst for H2 evolution from water-splitting.

As shown in Fig. 7c, the photoinduced charge separation efficiency between the LaFeO3, CQDs, and CdS in the ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite was investigated qualitatively by the photocurrent experiments and compared it with the LaFeO3/CQD, LaFeO3/CdS, and CdS/CQD nanocomposites. As noticed, the LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite exhibited a considerably large amount of photocurrent density compared to LaFeO3/CQD, LaFeO3/CdS, and CdS/CQD nanocomposites, proving that the effective charge separation between the LaFeO3, CQDs, and CdS, which further responsible for the significant photocatalytic performance of LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite.

To further confirm an efficient H2 evolution from the ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite and binary nanocomposites of LaFeO3/CQD, CdS/CQD, LaFeO3/CdS time-resolved PL was performed to determine the photo-induced charge carrier lifetime. As exhibited in Fig. 7d, the average PL lifetime of the LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite (23.6 ns) is longest when compared to that of binary LaFeO3/CdS (21.8 ns), CdS/CQD (18.2 ns), and LaFeO3/CQD (12.8 ns) nanocomposites. It can be observed that the fabrication of ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite using CQDs as an electron mediator quenched PL intensity and increased decay lifetime of charge carriers. These time-resolved PL results stated that the presence of CQDs in the LaFeO3/CdS nanocomposite contributed to the quick transfer and more lifetime separation of photoinduced electrons compared to that of the binary nanocomposites, leading to the decreases the chance of excitons recombination and utilization of more electrons in ‘H+’ reduction. Overall, the results suggest that an efficient interfacial charge transfer and more effective photo-induced carriers’ formation were occurred in the ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite due to the emergence of non-radiative quenching pathways.

Moreover, as displayed in Figure S3, the PXRD pattern of the used ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite remains the same under photocatalytic reaction conditions, which demonstrates that the synthesized ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite holds good structural stability and further it can be reused for H2 evolution from water splitting. Altogether, the designed ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite can be considered as an excellent, unique, affordable, potential and stable photocatalyst for H2 evolution from water-splitting.

On the basis of high HER over the LaFeO3/CdS and LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposites, a tentative photocatalytic hydrogen production mechanism was inferred. According to the Eqs. (1) and (2), the band potentials of CB and VB (ECB and EVB) are estimated and furnished in the supporting information.

The estimated EVB and ECB of LaFeO3 are 2.09 and −0.01 eV, and of CdS are 1.94 and −0.37 eV respectively. Although LaFeO3 has similar bandgap energy to CdS, as displayed in Fig. 8 a the negative value of CB of CS is higher than that of LaFeO3, which favors the photo-induced electrons can transport from CB of CdS to the CB of LaFeO3. It leads to more efficient charge separation in between these two semiconductors, which is responsible for more HER in LaFeO3/CdS binary nanocomposite (22,537 μmol h−1 gcat −1) compared to that of pristine LaFeO3 (42 μmol h−1 gcat −1) and pristine CdS (9,604 μmol h−1 gcat −1). Then, coming to the LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite as demonstrated in Fig. 8b, the fabricated CQDs acting as good co-catalysts, electron acceptors, and transporters, that’s why the photo-induced electrons in the CB of LaFeO3 could be successfully transported to CQDs and further involved in the reduction of H+ to H2, while the holes accumulated in the VB of CdS participated in the oxidation of lactic acid to pyruvic acid. In addition, the fabricated CQDs into the LaFeO3/CdS system possess photosensitizer and up conversion photoluminescence properties which enhance the light-harvesting and charge separation abilities of the material further which could lead to an improvement in HER of LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite up to 25,302 μmol h−1 gcat −1.

In summary, ternary LaFeO3/CdS/CQD nanocomposite was synthesized via hydrothermal treatment by fabricating CQDs into the LaFeO3/CdS binary nanocomposite. PXRD, FE-SEM, TEM, and XPS results confirmed the development of LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite with large interfacial interactions. The successful fabrication of CQDs efficaciously enhances the visible-light absorption characteristics and further extends the interfacial charge separation. More interestingly, upon sunlight illumination, LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite exhibited superior and outstanding photocatalytic performance for the H2 evolution with a rate of 25,302 μmol h−1 gcat −1 compared to the pristine LaFeO3, pristine CdS, and binary nanocomposites of LaFeO3/CQD, CdS/CQD, and LaFeO3/CdS. The remarkable improvement in the photocatalytic performance of this ternary nanocomposite corresponding to CQDs served as photosensitizers/co-catalysts to successfully improve the interfacial electron transfer in the composite, which further leads to the suppress in the photo-responsive excitons recombination. Hopefully, this uniquely developed stable LaFeO3/CdS/CQD ternary nanocomposite opens opportunities for the development of high potential photocatalytic systems to realize the abundant production of solar fuels.
